text:
- **[NMP #2864](https://github.com/pablof7z/nostr-multi-platform/issues/2864)** — Epic: NIP-60/61 wallet milestone. Six phases straight from the design doc: reactivate `nmp-nip60` → `nmp-wallet` composition crate (WalletBackend seam + operation journal) → NWC consolidation behind the seam → NIP-60 Cashu wallet → NIP-61 nutzap send/receive → Chirp UX. Plus the doc's invariants (fail-closed, no secrets in projections, thin shells) and its full test-gate list. [...] 2. **Binds one typed projection** (`"wallet"`) — backend readiness, capability flags, balances by mint/unit, pending operations by correlation id, bounded history. The projection **never** contains keys, Cashu proofs, or secrets. UI enables buttons off capability flags: if the backend can't melt to Lightning, there is no melt button — an absent capability, never a runtime error. [...] **Your app does exactly two things:**

1. **Dispatches typed actions** — `nmp.wallet.cashu.create`, `cashu.deposit_quote`, `nutzap.send`, `nutzap.redeem`, `pay_invoice`, … Fire-and-forget with a correlation id; no return values across FFI.
2. **Binds one typed projection** (`"wallet"`) — backend readiness, capability flags, balances by mint/unit, pending operations by correlation id, bounded history. The projection **never** contains keys, Cashu proofs, or secrets. UI enables buttons off capability flags: if the backend can't melt to Lightning, there is no melt button — an absent capability, never a runtime error.

**What the kernel does underneath, using "send a nutzap" as the example:** your action arrives → `nmp-wallet` resolves the recipient's kind:10019 (their accepted mints, relays, and P2PK pubkey) through normal kernel reads — and **fails closed as state** if they don't have one → selects your proofs, swaps them at the mint into P2PK-locked proofs (every value-moving step recorded first in a durable **operation journal**, so a process crash can never double-spend — on restart the kernel reconciles against the mint) → publishes the kind:9321 to *their* relays through the publish chokepoint → rolls over your token events (new 7375s + NIP-09 delete) → the projection updates → your screen re-renders. Receiving is the mirror image: the kernel subscribes for 9321s p-tagged to you, validates P2PK+DLEQ, swaps into your own proofs, then shows the balance change.

That's the whole builder experience: **actions in, projection out**. Everything with a private key, a proof, a mint URL decision, or a relay decision is on the Rust side of the line — which is also why the builder doc must never mention Cashu internals as things the app manipulates (and per ADR-0075, never mention Trellis at all). [...] **What the kernel does underneath, using "send a nutzap" as the example:** your action arrives → `nmp-wallet` resolves the recipient's kind:10019 (their accepted mints, relays, and P2PK pubkey) through normal kernel reads — and **fails closed as state** if they don't have one → selects your proofs, swaps them at the mint into P2PK-locked proofs (every value-moving step recorded first in a durable **operation journal**, so a process crash can never double-spend — on restart the kernel reconciles against the mint) → publishes the kind:9321 to *their* relays through the publish chokepoint → rolls over your token events (new 7375s + NIP-09 delete) → the projection updates → your screen re-renders. Receiving is the mirror image: the kernel subscribes for 9321s p-tagged to you, validates P2PK+DLEQ, swaps into your own proofs, then shows the balance change. [...] **The high-level model** (above): a NIP-60 wallet app on NMP is *actions in, projection out*. The app dispatches `nmp.wallet.*` typed actions and binds the `"wallet"` projection; the Rust kernel owns everything dangerous — Cashu proofs and keys, the mint HTTP round-trips, relay resolution (kind:10019 → NIP-65), the durable operation journal that makes crashes double-spend-safe, and fail-closed handling when a recipient can't receive. Capability flags drive the UI: a feature the backend lacks is a missing button, never an error dialog.
